title: Home
userGoal: Review a public-facing website starter and choose the next route.

props.headline: Public website prototypes without internal tool clutter.
props.body: A compact static website starter with reusable blocks for landing pages, pricing, about pages, contact, proof, content, and conversion.

props.label: Website kit
props.body: The default block range now focuses on public-facing SaaS, agency, product, and content websites.

props.title: Familiar public-site proof patterns
props.body: Use neutral logo rows for customers, partners, integrations, press, or internal credibility without decorative noise.

props.title: Everything a public website needs.
props.body: The system covers conversion, trust, pricing, content, company, contact, and utility routes.

props.title: Distinct blocks for distinct website jobs
props.body: Avoid one repeated card pattern. Each section carries a different public-site role.

items.title: Conversion
items.body: Hero, CTA, banner, newsletter, contact, and pricing sections.

items.title: Trust
items.body: Logo clouds, social proof, testimonials, stats, and case-study summaries.

items.title: Content
items.body: Article, rich text, resource lists, steps, values, and team sections.

props.title: Prototype the common public page flow
props.body: Use tabbed sections to compare content angles while keeping the first pass compact.

tabs.label: Landing
tabs.title: Lead with value and proof
tabs.body: Combine hero, proof, feature, CTA, and FAQ sections for first-impression review.

tabs.label: Company
tabs.title: Show credibility with public pages
tabs.body: Use values, team, resources, and rich text to shape public company pages.

tabs.label: Conversion
tabs.title: Answer decision questions
tabs.body: Pair pricing, comparison, FAQ, testimonial, and contact blocks.

props.title: A product artifact can still support a public page
props.body: Public pages need concrete proof, but the proof should serve the buying story.

items.title: No app shell required
items.body: Default routes are Home, Pricing, About, Contact, and 404.

items.title: Content stays editable
items.body: Page JSON owns copy and sample data for each public section.

items.title: Static output stays simple
items.body: Generated HTML remains portable and GitHub Pages friendly.

props.title: Use quantified trust without visual noise
props.body: A public-facing prototype should make credibility easy to scan in the first review.

props.quote: The starter now feels like a real public website system.
props.author: Draftpine reviewer

props.title: Public proof variants
props.body: Use single quotes for emphasis or grids when the page needs multiple audience signals.

items.quote: We can review the homepage story before touching production code.
items.author: Product lead

items.quote: The pricing and FAQ sections make decision pages easier to critique.
items.author: Growth lead

items.quote: The about page blocks keep company content structured.
items.author: Brand lead

props.title: Integration and ecosystem grids
props.body: Use this for partners, tools, platforms, marketplaces, or public compatibility pages.

items.name: GitHub
items.body: Source-linked prototypes and public deploy previews.

props.title: Content and resource blocks
props.body: Round out public websites with articles, guides, changelogs, and resource cards.

items.title: Landing page review checklist
items.body: Evaluate hierarchy, proof, offer, FAQ, and conversion paths.

items.title: Website block system
items.body: New public-facing sections for company and marketing sites.

items.title: Pricing page starter
items.body: Plans, comparison, proof, FAQ, and contact handoff.

props.title: Launch teams can review the public story first
props.body: Use a featured case-study block when a homepage needs one concrete customer narrative before the final CTA.

props.title: Case study grids
props.body: Use grids when the page needs multiple public examples without full long-form stories.

items.title: Pricing review
items.body: A team aligned on plans, FAQ, and contact routing before implementation.

items.title: Launch page
items.body: A service business tested offer, proof, and inquiry flow in a static prototype.

items.title: Resource hub
items.body: A publisher mapped article and guide cards before writing final copy.

props.title: Press and quote strips
props.body: Short external proof works best when it is compact and scannable.

items.quote: A focused static pass for public-site reviews.
Plain HTML output keeps the prototype easy to inspect.
The route model makes sitemap conversations concrete.

props.title: From prompt to public prototype
props.body: Keep the workflow oriented around website review, not internal tool state.

items.title: Describe the offer
items.body: Name the audience, promise, proof, and primary conversion action.

items.title: Choose public blocks
items.body: Assemble hero, proof, features, pricing, FAQ, and contact.

items.title: Review static routes
items.body: Generate the site, inspect desktop and mobile, then iterate on content.

props.title: Public website starter questions
props.body: These are the decisions a website prototype should clarify early.

items.question: Can I still build app screens?
items.answer: Yes. Generic app-like blocks remain available, but the default starter focuses on public websites.

items.question: What routes should a public starter include?
items.answer: Home, Pricing, About, Contact, and a 404 route cover most website review flows.

items.question: Can content-heavy pages be represented?
items.answer: Yes. Article, rich text, resources, values, team, and steps cover public content pages.

props.title: Need to map more public pages?
props.body: Add resources, careers, changelog, legal, or customer story routes when the website needs them.

props.title: Prototype the public site before the build
props.body: Use the website-first starter as a shared review surface for product, design, and marketing.

props.title: Start with a public page, then add only what the site needs.
props.body: The default block range is broad enough for modern product sites without pulling the prototype toward internal tools.
